verses.text: It came to pass within a while after, in the time of wheat harvest, that Samson visited his wife with a kid; and he said, I will go in to my wife into the chamber. But her father would not suffer him to go in. Her father said, I verily thought that thou hadst utterly hated her; therefore I gave her to thy companion: is not her younger sister fairer than she? take her, I pray thee, instead of her. Guidance: Samson cools down enough to go reclaim his wife — with a peace offering of a young goat. The father, surprised to see him, has already given her to someone else and tries to substitute the younger sister, as if the daughter were interchangeable. Both men are casual about a woman the chapter will not even name. The bride is a chess piece between two pride-driven negotiators. The next chapter's catastrophe is set up by the father's attempt to manage Samson's anger with another daughter.

It came to pass within a while after, in the time of wheat harvest, that Samson visited his wife with a kid; and he said, I will go in to my wife into the chamber. But her father would not suffer him to go in. Her father said, I verily thought that thou hadst utterly hated her; therefore I gave her to thy companion: is not her younger sister fairer than she? take her, I pray thee, instead of her. Guidance: Samson cools down enough to go reclaim his wife — with a peace offering of a young goat. The father, surprised to see him, has already given her to someone else and tries to substitute the younger sister, as if the daughter were interchangeable. Both men are casual about a woman the chapter will not even name. The bride is a chess piece between two pride-driven negotiators. The next chapter's catastrophe is set up by the father's attempt to manage Samson's anger with another daughter.

Samson said concerning them, Now shall I be more blameless than the Philistines, though I do them a displeasure. Samson went and caught three hundred foxes, and took firebrands, and turned tail to tail, and put a firebrand in the midst between two tails. When he had set the brands on fire, he let them go into the standing corn of the Philistines, and burnt up both the shocks, and also the standing corn, with the vineyards and olives. Guidance: 'More blameless than the Philistines' — Samson absolves himself in advance. He rationalizes before he retaliates. Then a comic-grotesque revenge: three hundred foxes (probably jackals), tied in pairs by their tails with torches lit between them, released into the wheat fields at harvest time. The agricultural calendar makes the damage maximal — shocks of harvested grain plus standing wheat plus vineyards plus olive groves. The economic basis of Philistia goes up in smoke. The Nazirite, who was not even to touch a grape, has now burned every grape in town.

Then the Philistines said, Who hath done this? And they answered, Samson, the son in law of the Timnite, because he had taken his wife, and given her to his companion. And the Philistines came up, and burnt her and her father with fire. Guidance: The Philistines bypass Samson and burn the woman and her father — the very thing the chaperones had threatened her with in 14:15 if she did not betray Samson's riddle. She betrayed him to avoid being burned alive; the betrayal did not save her. The vengeance cycle catches her in the middle. The book of Judges keeps placing women at the receiving end of men's vendettas. The Philistines could have come for Samson; they chose softer targets. Cruelty looks for the easier death.

Samson said unto them, Though ye have done this, yet will I be avenged of you, and after that I will cease. He smote them hip and thigh with a great slaughter: and he went down and dwelt in the top of the rock Etam. Guidance: Samson's reply is the cycle in two sentences: 'though ye have done this' (acknowledging) and 'I will be avenged' (escalating). 'Hip and thigh' is a Hebrew idiom meaning total, no-mercy violence. Then he retreats to a cave at the rock Etam. Anger that has been satisfied still needs a hideout — vengeance always changes the avenger's address. The next move, the Philistine army marching into Judah after him, will draw an entire tribe into the orbit of his personal feud.

Then the Philistines went up, and pitched in Judah, and spread themselves in Lehi. And the men of Judah said, Why are ye come up against us? And they answered, To bind Samson are we come up, to do to him as he hath done to us. Guidance: The Philistines occupy Lehi (which means 'jawbone' — the chapter's prophetic place-name). 'Why are ye come up against us?' is the wrong question to ask in v9 — Judah is by now so accustomed to Philistine dominion that they treat the occupation as routine. The next verse will show what they think of their own deliverer.

Then the Philistines went up, and pitched in Judah, and spread themselves in Lehi. And the men of Judah said, Why are ye come up against us? And they answered, To bind Samson are we come up, to do to him as he hath done to us. Guidance: The Philistines occupy Lehi (which means 'jawbone' — the chapter's prophetic place-name). 'Why are ye come up against us?' is the wrong question to ask in v9 — Judah is by now so accustomed to Philistine dominion that they treat the occupation as routine. The next verse will show what they think of their own deliverer.

Then three thousand men of Judah went to the top of the rock Etam, and said to Samson, Knowest thou not that the Philistines are rulers over us? what is this that thou hast done unto us? He said unto them, As they did unto me, so have I done unto them. They said unto him, We are come down to bind thee, that we may deliver thee into the hand of the Philistines. Samson said unto them, Swear unto me, that ye will not fall upon me yourselves. They spake unto him, saying, No; but we will bind thee fast, and deliver thee into their hand: but surely we will not kill thee. They bound him with two new cords, and brought him up from the rock. Guidance: Three thousand men of Judah come — not to fight the Philistines, but to arrest their own deliverer. 'Knowest thou not the Philistines are rulers over us?' They have accepted the yoke and resent the man who keeps disturbing it. Samson lets himself be bound on the condition his own brothers won't kill him. The chapter that began with Samson smoking out Philistia ends with Israel handing him over. The deliverer is more inconvenient than the oppressor. A Christ-foreshadow — bound and handed over by his own people to the Gentile occupiers; the parallel is sharp and intentional in early-Christian reading.

When he came unto Lehi, the Philistines shouted against him: and the Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and the cords that were upon his arms became as flax that was burnt with fire, and his bands loosed from off his hands. He found a new jawbone of an ass, and put forth his hand, and took it, and slew a thousand men therewith. Guidance: The Philistine cheer rises; the Spirit of the LORD comes down. The two new ropes burn off like flax (literally 'as flax that has caught fire from a flame'). Samson finds a fresh donkey jawbone — fresh, because a dried one would have shattered — and one Nazirite-defiling corpse-touch becomes the day's weapon. He kills a thousand men with the jawbone of an ass. The text wants us to notice: the deliverer who was bound by his own brothers and meant for death is unbreakable when the Spirit comes. No prison is permanent under that anointing.

The two new ropes burn off like flax (literally 'as flax that has caught fire from a flame'). Samson said, With the jawbone of an ass, heaps upon heaps, with the jaw of an ass have I slain a thousand men. It came to pass, when he had made an end of speaking, that he cast away the jawbone out of his hand, and called that place Ramath-lehi. Guidance: A Hebrew pun — the word for 'ass' (hamor) and the word for 'heaps' (homer) sound nearly identical. Samson composes a victory taunt on the spot: 'with the jaw of an ass, ass-heaps upon ass-heaps.' Witty, but the LORD's name is absent. Compare Deborah's song (Judges 5) which began with 'praise ye the LORD.' Samson sings about himself. The narrative is letting his pride do its own self-portrait. Then he names the place 'Ramath-lehi' — 'jawbone height.' He memorializes the weapon, not the Giver.

Samson said, With the jawbone of an ass, heaps upon heaps, with the jaw of an ass have I slain a thousand men. It came to pass, when he had made an end of speaking, that he cast away the jawbone out of his hand, and called that place Ramath-lehi. Guidance: A Hebrew pun — the word for 'ass' (hamor) and the word for 'heaps' (homer) sound nearly identical. Samson composes a victory taunt on the spot: 'with the jaw of an ass, ass-heaps upon ass-heaps.' Witty, but the LORD's name is absent. Compare Deborah's song (Judges 5) which began with 'praise ye the LORD.' Samson sings about himself. The narrative is letting his pride do its own self-portrait. Then he names the place 'Ramath-lehi' — 'jawbone height.' He memorializes the weapon, not the Giver.

He was sore athirst, and called on the LORD, and said, Thou hast given this great deliverance into the hand of thy servant: and now shall I die for thirst, and fall into the hand of the uncircumcised? But God clave an hollow place that was in the jaw, and there came water thereout; and when he had drunk, his spirit came again, and he revived: wherefore he called the name thereof En-hakkore, which is in Lehi unto this day. Guidance: Now Samson prays. The boast of v16 melts into thirst, and finally the LORD is named. 'Thy servant' — the right title at last. The prayer is partly self-pitying ('shall I die for thirst?') but it is real, and God answers. Water from a 'hollow place' — perhaps a hollow in the jawbone (KJV) or, more likely, in the rock at Lehi. He drinks; his spirit returns; he names the spring En-hakkore — 'the spring of him who called.' The chapter's two place-names, side by side, tell the chapter's lesson: the weapon got remembered first ('jawbone height'), but the prayer is what gave him another breath ('spring of the one who called'). Faith arrives in the last verse where it should have been in the first.

He judged Israel in the days of the Philistines twenty years. Guidance: Twenty years — twice the tenure of most minor judges. And the strange phrase 'in the days of the Philistines.' Samson did not end the Philistine era; he lived within it. His judging is not described in the usual term-of-peace language. He held the role for two decades while Israel remained under Philistine dominion. He kept the people's identity alive without freeing the people's land. Sometimes the LORD's work in an era is to preserve, not to deliver outright. David will finish what Samson only kept alive.
